title: Temperature Mapping of Warehouses and Storage
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A senior QP's guide to temperature mapping of warehouses and storage: protocol design, sensor placement, ALCOA+ data, excursions and requalification under GDP.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, with a single subtle green accent. In sharp foreground focus, a calibrated temperature data logger and a slim sensor probe rest on a stainless-steel rack edge inside a climate-controlled warehouse, fine condensation beading on a nearby glass surface. Behind, gently blurred with a shallow depth of field, tall pharmaceutical racking and a row of monitored cold-storage units recede into soft cyan light, with a faint HVAC vent glow overhead. The mood is precise, trustworthy and premium, with clean reflective floors and a quiet sense of controlled environment. Soft natural light, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: How many temperature sensors do I need for a mapping study?
faqs.a: There is no single fixed number; it is driven by the volume and complexity of the space and justified through quality risk management. A large ambient warehouse typically needs a three-dimensional grid covering corners, the centre and high and low racking, whereas a small qualified fridge needs far fewer sensors but must still capture the true extremes. Document the rationale for both the count and the placement in your approved protocol.

faqs.q: How often should warehouse temperature mapping be repeated?
faqs.a: Mapping should be repeated at defined, risk-based intervals and, just as importantly, whenever a relevant change occurs. Triggers include altered racking or partitioning, changes to HVAC capacity, significant shifts in throughput or storage density, and unexplained excursion trends. Tying requalification to your change control and self-inspection programmes ensures the study stays representative rather than becoming a one-off certificate.

faqs.q: Does temperature mapping need to cover summer and winter?
faqs.a: Ideally yes, because external climate strongly influences a building's internal thermal profile. Best practice is to map across both summer and winter extremes, or to provide a documented scientific rationale linking a single study to worst-case external conditions. Mapping only in mild weather is a frequent inspection weakness, as it fails to demonstrate control during a heatwave or a hard frost.
